source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 10.3.1 cpio copy-out mode (pp. 186-188)

questions.q: What are cpio's three operating modes?
options: copy-out (-o, create), copy-in (-i, extract), copy-pass (-p, copy dir to dir)
questions.why: copy-out (-o) creates an archive, copy-in (-i) extracts, copy-pass (-p) copies files between directories.

questions.q: In copy-out mode, where does cpio get its list of files to back up?
options: From standard input - you pipe a list from find, ls or cat into it
It backs up the whole disk automatically
questions.why: copy-out reads the file list from stdin; commonly 'find ... | cpio -o'. (ls for this must be a plain list - no -l or -R.)

questions.q: Back up the /etc directory (recursively) to /backups/etc.cpio using find piped into cpio, with output redirection.
questions.why: find lists the files, the pipe feeds them to cpio -o (v = verbose), and > writes the archive.

questions.q: Do the same backup of /etc to /backups/etc.cpio, but use cpio's -F option instead of the > redirection.
questions.why: -F names the archive medium, replacing the > redirection (and can also replace < when reading).

questions.q: What determines whether a cpio backup is relative or absolute?
options: The path given to find: an absolute path (find /etc) gives an absolute backup; a relative path (find etc) gives a relative one
questions.why: cpio records whatever find outputs, so 'cd /; find etc' is relative while 'find /etc' is absolute.

questions.q: Append /etc/shadow to the existing cpio archive SystemFiles.A.cpio.
questions.why: -A appends to an existing archive (direct-access media only); -F designates the archive to modify.

questions.q: If you use ls (instead of find) to build the file list for cpio, what is the restriction?
options: It must be a simple list of names - no -l (details) and no -R (recursive)
ls cannot be used at all
ls must be run as root
ls must include -l for cpio to parse it
questions.why: cpio needs a plain list of names, so ls must not use -l or -R.
